content.problemStatement:
Write a program that does the following



Declare an integer variable  **`num`**  and initialize it to a user defined input
Output to the console the factorial of  **`num`**

Remember to use  `loops`  for this problem
Factorial of a number  `n`  is the product of all the numbers from 1 to  `n`
Factorial of a number(n) =  `n × (n-1) × ... 2 × 1`
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer num (0 ≤ num ≤ 20).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line exactly as: "The factorial of the given number is: X" where X is the factorial of num.

sampleTestCases.output: The factorial of the given number is: 720
sampleTestCases.explanation: There is one test case with num = 6. 6! = 6×5×4×3×2×1 = 720.

sampleTestCases.output: The factorial of the given number is: 1
The factorial of the given number is: 6\nThe factorial of the given number is: 120\nThe factorial of the given number is: 3628800

hidden.label: minimum boundary
hidden.expectedOutput: The factorial of the given number is: 1

hidden.label: maximum boundary
hidden.expectedOutput: The factorial of the given number is: 2432902008176640000

hidden.label: edge case
hidden.expectedOutput: The factorial of the given number is: 1\nThe factorial of the given number is: 2\nThe factorial of the given number is: 6

hidden.label: general case 1
hidden.expectedOutput: The factorial of the given number is: 5040\nThe factorial of the given number is: 1307674368000

hidden.label: general case 2
hidden.expectedOutput:
The factorial of the given number is: 24
The factorial of the given number is: 120
The factorial of the given number is: 720
The factorial of the given number is: 3628800

solution.approach: For each test case we compute the factorial of the given number using an iterative loop. Starting with result = 1, multiply result by every integer i from 2 to num inclusive. The loop runs in O(num) time, which is at most 20 iterations, so it is trivial. Using an unsigned 64‑bit integer (or BigInt in JavaScript) safely stores all values up to 20!. The algorithm is repeated for all T test cases.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N) where N is the value of num (N ≤ 20). In practice this is O(T) because N is bounded by a constant.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space per test case (only a few scalar variables).
